event: Polynesian voyagers, likely from Samoa and Tonga, first settle Niue, establishing the island's earliest human communities.
Niuean society develops a distinctive culture with no single paramount chief, organized instead around village-based kin groups and matai-style leaders.
A line of patu-iki (kings) begins to emerge, providing intermittent island-wide leadership amid largely decentralized governance.
Captain James Cook sights Niue and attempts to land three times but is repelled by hostile islanders, prompting him to name it the 'Savage Island.'
Missionaries of the London Missionary Society attempt to land but are initially turned away by wary Niueans.
Peniamina, a Niuean trained by missionaries in Samoa, returns to begin spreading Christianity on the island.
Samoan teacher Paulo arrives and successfully establishes Christianity, leading to widespread conversion across Niue.
George Lawes of the London Missionary Society becomes the first resident European missionary on Niue.
Mataio Tuitonga is recognized as the first formally elected patu-iki, beginning a continuous line of Niuean kings.
King Fataaiki petitions Queen Victoria seeking British protection for Niue against foreign annexation.
Britain declares Niue a protectorate after renewed requests from Niuean leaders, including King Togia-Pulu-toaki.
Niue is annexed by Britain and then formally placed under the administration of New Zealand, initially as part of the Cook Islands.
Niue is separated administratively from the Cook Islands and given its own resident commissioner and island council.
The global influenza pandemic reaches Niue, causing significant deaths among the island population.
Members of the Maori Battalion's Niuean contingent and labour service support the Allied war effort during the Pacific theatre of World War II.
A new Niue Assembly is established, expanding local self-government under New Zealand administration.
Robert Rex, a future leader of Niue, rises to prominence in island politics as movement toward greater autonomy grows.
Niue becomes a self-governing state in free association with New Zealand, with Robert Rex as its first premier.
Hanan International Airport opens, greatly improving Niue's connections to the outside world.
Premier Robert Rex, who had led Niue since self-government, dies in office after nearly two decades of leadership.
Frank Lui becomes premier, continuing the development of Niue's self-governing institutions.
Niue becomes one of the first territories in the world to offer free island-wide wireless internet access to its residents.
Cyclone Heta devastates Niue, destroying much of the capital Alofi and prompting concerns about the island's long-term viability.
Population decline accelerates as emigration to New Zealand leaves Niue with roughly 1,500 residents, far below its mid-century peak.
Niue, along with New Zealand, becomes a founding signatory in regional climate initiatives addressing threats to low-lying Pacific communities.
Niue remains COVID-19 free through strict border controls, leveraging its isolation during the global pandemic.
Niue partners with conservation groups to designate a vast marine protected area, safeguarding much of its exclusive economic zone.
The United States formally establishes diplomatic relations with Niue, recognizing it as a sovereign and independent state.
